reasoning: Pine tree is inanimate ending in ʔ. Plural hiseto? follows pattern replace ʔ with t + o?. Locative follows pattern replace o? with e? for this class (similar to tooth beicito -> beicte?, leg ooto -> oote?). Thus hiset e?.
Leg plural ooto, locative oote?. Matches class of nouns ending in ʔ (like pine tree hiseʔ -> hiseto?). Singular ends in ʔ, plural replace ʔ with t + o?.
Enemy singular coox, animate ending in ox. Matches bag ceʔinox (inanimate) -> ceʔino? pattern: delete final x + o?. Thus coo?.
Enemy animate ending ox. For animate, obviative adds -n after deleting x (analogous to woman hisei -> hisen, i -> en; man hinen -> hinan, en -> an). Thus coo + n = coon.
Enemy animate. Locative for animate unpossessed: singular (adjusted by deleting x to coo) + ewe? (like woman hisei + ewe? -> hiseiewe?; man hinen + ewe? -> hinenewe?). Thus coo + ewe? = cooewe?.
Bag inanimate ending ox. Obviative for inanimate: delete x + w (like car hoti -> hotiw). Thus ceʔin + w = ceʔinw.
Bag inanimate, plural ceʔino?. Locative for this class (ending ox, plural o?): replace o? with e? (consistent with no contraction needed). Thus ceʔin + e? = ceʔine?.
Pants inanimate ending oo. Plural follows default inanimate -no? (like book class -no, woman class but adjusted for inanimate; consistent with O + o? pattern where O wotoon).

Pants inanimate. Obviative for inanimate: similar to coon but for I, adjust to -on? No, pattern O derived such that P = O + o? (like car hotiw + o? = hotiwo?; woman hisen + o? = hiseino?). With P wotoono?, O = wotoon.
Book inanimate, plural woθonohe no, locative woθonohe ne?. Pattern for this class: singular ends in vowel e or no marker, plural + no, locative + ne?. Thus base woθonohe.
Book inanimate ending e. Obviative for inanimate: add w at end (like car hoti -> hotiw; bag ceʔinw). Thus woθonohe + w = woθonohew.
Eagle animate ending i (class like older sister but unpossessed). Obviative niʔehio suggests final i -> io. Thus singular niʔehi.
Eagle animate ending i with C=h before i. Plural: i -> ho? (like older sister nebi (C=b) -> nebho?; adjusted for h: h i -> h ho?). Thus niʔeh + ho? = niʔehho?.
Eagle animate ending i, C=h. Locative: i -> hewe? (like older sister nebi -> nebhewe?, b i -> b hewe?; for h: h i -> h hewe?). Thus niʔeh + hewe? = niʔehhewe?.
Block inanimate ending es. Obviative: delete s + o (pattern for -es class, analogous to other inanimate changes like ʔ -> t but for s -> o). Thus ceʔibe + o = ceʔibeo.
Block plural ceʔbexo. Locative for -es class (plural -xo): replace o with e? (consistent with -to? -> -te? class). Thus ceʔbex + e? = ceʔbexe?.
Arm ends es (body part, but has obviative). Plural for -es class: delete i (if present) or adjust, s -> x + o (like block ceʔibes -> ceʔbexo, ib es -> b ex o; ben es -> ben ex o).
Arm ends es. Obviative same as block pattern: delete s + o. Thus bene + o = beneo.
Arm plural benexo. Locative: replace o with e? (same as block). Thus benex + e? = benexe?.
Uncle possessed animate like older sister nebi. Plural nesho?. Root shi, singular n- e- shi (epenthetic e) = neshi; plural n e sh o? (i -> o?, sh h -> sh).
Uncle possessed animate. Obviative: hi- + root shi + io (like older sister hi- + bi + io = hibio). Thus hi sh i o = hishio.
Uncle possessed animate. Locative: n- + root sh + hewe? (like older sister n- + b + hewe? = nebhewe?; sh + hewe? = sh ewe?, with e from epenthetic). Thus ne + sh ewe? = neshewe?.

answer: The forms follow Algonquian-style morphological patterns differentiated by animacy, possession, and stem ending. Classes include: -i animate unpossessed (e.g., woman: P -no?, O -en, L -ewe?); -i with specific C (e.g., h/b: P -ho?, O -io, L -hewe?); ending ʔ/θ inanimate/body (P -to?, L -te?); -ox (P -o?, O -n(A)/-w(I), L -e?(I)/-ewe?(A)); -es (P -xo, O -o, L -exe?); -u animate (P insert h + u?, O -uo, L ...huwe?); -oo inanimate (P -no?, O -on, L -he? special); -e inanimate (P -no, O -w, L -ne?); possessed kinship (singular n- + root + i/e, P ...ho?, O hi- + root + io, L n- + root + hewe?). Shaded cells (hand, leg, tooth) lack obviative (inalienable body parts). Patterns ensure consistency across similar nouns.
reasoning: Analyzed given forms for suffixes/changes by grouping nouns by animacy (A/I), possession (yes/no), endings (-i, -u, -ʔ, -θ, -ox, -es, -oo, -e). Derived rules from examples (e.g., plural often O + o? where applicable; locative varies by class: -ewe? animate, -e? certain inanimate/body). Inferred missing by analogy, ensuring unique fits without external data.
